title: Magnetic force on moving charges
description: Let's practice some problems to better understand how to calculate the magnetic force on a charge that's moving in a magnetic field.

assessment_items.question_content:
A $0.10\ \text T$ magnetic field points in the $+y$ direction. A $-2.0\ \text C$ point charge enters the region with a speed of $3.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$, making a $60\degree$ angle with the $x$-axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the force experienced by the point charge?**    
*Note: The $+z$ direction points out of the screen, $\bigodot$.*

($\text a$) Magnitude $=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text N$

($\text b$) Direction:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

The velocity vector, $\bold\blue v$, makes an angle, $\purpleD\theta=\purpleD{30\degree}$, with the magnetic field, $\bold\redD B$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The force on the charge is the vector, $\bold F$, given as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold\blue v\times\bold\redD B)
\end{align}$

##Solution

**Magnitude:**

$\begin{align}
|\bold F|&=|q||\bold\blue v||\bold\redD B|\sin\purpleD\theta\\\\
&=(2.0\ \text C)\cdot\left(\blue{3.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}}\right)\cdot(\redD{0.10\ \text T})\cdot\sin\purpleD{30\degree}\\\\
&=0.30\ \text N
\end{align}$

**Direction:**    

*Right-hand rule:* With our right hand, let's point the -

- index finger along $\bold\blue v$,

- middle-finger along $\bold\redD B$.

The stretched thumb gives the direction of the cross-product, $\bold\blue v\times\bold\redD B$, $+z$ in this case. 

Since $q$ is negative, $\bold F$ points in the $-z$ direction.

The correct answer is:

Magnitude: $\boxed{0.30}\ \text N$

Direction: $-z$

answers.message: The magnitude of a vector is a positive quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
A $0.30\ \text T$ magnetic field points in the $-x$ direction. A $-1.5\ \text C$ point charge enters the region with a speed of $4.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$, making a $30\degree$ angle with the $x$-axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the force experienced by the point charge?**    
*Note: The $+z$ direction points out of the screen, $\bigodot$.*

($\text a$) Magnitude $=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text N$

($\text b$) Direction:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

The velocity vector, $\bold\blue v$, makes an angle, $\purpleD\theta=\purpleD{150\degree}$, with the magnetic field, $\bold\redD B$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The force on the charge is the vector, $\bold F$, given as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold\blue v\times\bold\redD B)
\end{align}$

##Solution

**Magnitude:**

$\begin{align}
|\bold F|&=|q||\bold\blue v||\bold\redD B|\sin\purpleD\theta\\\\
&=(1.5\ \text C)\cdot\left(\blue{4.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}}\right)\cdot(\redD{0.30\ \text T})\cdot\sin\purpleD{150\degree}\\\\
&=0.90\ \text N
\end{align}$

**Direction:**    

*Right-hand rule:* With our right hand, let's point the -

- index finger along $\bold\blue v$,

- middle-finger along $\bold\redD B$.

The stretched thumb gives the direction of the cross-product, $\bold\blue v\times\bold\redD B$, $+z$ in this case. 

Since $q$ is negative, $\bold F$ points in the $-z$ direction.

The correct answer is:

Magnitude: $\boxed{0.90}\ \text N$

Direction: $-z$

answers.message: The magnitude of a vector is a positive quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
A region of uniform magnetic field makes a circular cross-section on the $x$-$y$ plane. The field points in the $+z$ direction (out of the screen).

[[☃ image 1]]

A $2.5\ \mu\text C$ point charge moves on the $x$-$y$ plane with a speed of $8.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$. It experiences a force of magnitude $4.0\ \mu\text N$ the instant it enters the magnetic field.

**What is the magnitude of the magnetic field?**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text T$
hints:
##Strategy

The velocity of the point charge, $\bold v$, is perpendicular to the magnetic field, $\bold B$.

The force on the charge is the vector, $\bold F$, given as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold v\times\bold B)
\end{align}$

##Solution

$\begin{align}
|\bold F|&=|q||\bold v||\bold B|\sin\theta\\\\
\Rightarrow |\bold B|&=\dfrac{|\bold\redD F|}{|\purpleD q||\bold\blue v|\sin\maroonD\theta}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redD{4.0\times10^{-6}\ \text N}}{(\purpleD{2.5\times10^{-6}\ \text C})\cdot\blue{8.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}}\cdot\sin\maroonD{90\degree}}\\\\
&=0.20\ \text T
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$\boxed{0.20}\ \text T$

answers.message: The magnitude of a vector is a positive quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
A region of uniform magnetic field makes a rectangular cross-section on the $x$-$y$ plane. The field points in the $-z$ direction (into the screen).

[[☃ image 1]]

A $0.50\ \mu\text C$ point charge moves on the $x$-$y$ plane with a speed of $6.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$. It experiences a force of magnitude $1.2\ \mu\text N$ the instant it enters the magnetic field.

**What is the magnitude of the magnetic field?**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text T$
hints:
##Strategy

The velocity of the point charge, $\bold v$, is perpendicular to the magnetic field, $\bold B$.

The force on the charge is the vector, $\bold F$, given as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold v\times\bold B)
\end{align}$

##Solution

$\begin{align}
|\bold F|&=|q||\bold v||\bold B|\sin\theta\\\\
\Rightarrow |\bold B|&=\dfrac{|\bold\redD F|}{|\purpleD q||\bold\blue v|\sin\maroonD\theta}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redD{1.2\times10^{-6}\ \text N}}{(\purpleD{0.50\times10^{-6}\ \text C})\cdot\blue{6.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}}\cdot\sin\maroonD{90\degree}}\\\\
&=0.40\ \text T
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$\boxed{0.40}\ \text T$

answers.message: The magnitude of a vector is a positive quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
A uniform magnetic field of magnitude, $0.50\ \text T$, lies on the $x$-$y$ plane making a $60\degree$ angle with the $x$-axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

When a $1.2\ \mu\text C$ point charge moves in the $+y$ direction, it experiences a force of magnitude, $3.0\ \mu\text N$.

**What is the speed of the charged particle?**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$
hints:
##Strategy

The velocity vector, $\bold\blue v$, makes an angle, $\purpleD\theta=\purpleD{30\degree}$, with the magnetic field, $\bold\redD B$.

[[☃ image 2]]

The force on the charge is the vector, $\bold F$, given as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold\blue v\times\bold\redD B)
\end{align}$

##Solution

$\begin{align}
|\bold F|&=|q||\bold v||\bold B|\sin\theta\\\\
\Rightarrow |\bold v|&=\dfrac{|\bold\purpleD F|}{|q||\bold\redD B|\sin\maroonD\theta}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{3.0\times10^{-6}\ \text N}}{(1.2\times10^{-6})\cdot\redD{0.50\ \text T}\cdot\sin\maroonD{30\degree}}\\\\
&=10\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$\boxed{10}\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A uniform magnetic field of magnitude, $0.15\ \text T$, lies on the $x$-$y$ plane making a $120\degree$ angle with the $x$-axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

When a $1.8\ \mu\text C$ point charge moves in the $+y$ direction, it experiences a force of magnitude, $2.7\ \mu\text N$.

**What is the speed of the charged particle?**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$
hints:
##Strategy

The velocity vector, $\bold\blue v$, makes an angle, $\purpleD\theta=\purpleD{30\degree}$, with the magnetic field, $\bold\redD B$.

[[☃ image 2]]

The force on the charge is the vector, $\bold F$, given as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold\blue v\times\bold\redD B)
\end{align}$

##Solution

$\begin{align}
|\bold F|&=|q||\bold v||\bold B|\sin\theta\\\\
\Rightarrow |\bold v|&=\dfrac{|\bold\purpleD F|}{|q||\bold\redD B|\sin\maroonD\theta}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{2.7\times10^{-6}\ \text N}}{(1.8\times10^{-6})\cdot\redD{0.15\ \text T}\cdot\sin\maroonD{30\degree}}\\\\
&=20\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$\boxed{20}\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A point charge, moving *east*, enters a region of uniform magnetic field with a speed of $5.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$. It immediately experiences a $1.5\ \text{mN}$ force in the *upward* direction.

The magnetic field has a magnitude, $0.30\ \text{mT}$, toward *north*.

**What is the charge of the particle?**    
*Note: $\text{mN}=$ millinewtons, $\text{mT}=$ milliteslas.  [[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text C$
hints:
##Strategy

The force on the charge, $\bold F$, is related to the charge, $q$, its velocity, $\bold v$, and the magnetic field, $\bold B$, as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold v\times\bold B)
\end{align}$

Let's note that:

- $\bold v$ is along east and $\bold B$ is along north, so $\bold v\perp\bold B$.

- $q$ is positive if $\bold F$ is in the same direction as the vector product, $\bold v\times\bold B$, and negative if $\bold F$ points opposite to $\bold v\times\bold B$.

##Solution

**Magnitude of $q$:**

$\begin{align}
|q|&=\dfrac{|\bold F|}{|\bold v\times\bold B|}\\\\
&=\dfrac{|\bold\redD F|}{|\bold\blue v||\bold\purpleD B|\sin90\degree}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redD{1.5\times10^{-3}\ \text N}}{\blue{5.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}}\cdot\purpleD{0.30\times10^{-3}\ \text T}}\\\\
&=1.0\ \text C
\end{align}$

We still need to check if $q$ is $+1.0\ \text C$ or $-1.0\ \text C$.

**Sign of $q$:**

*Right-hand rule:* With our right hand, let's point the -

- index finger along $\bold v$ (*east*),

- middle-finger along $\bold B$ (*north*).

The stretched thumb gives the direction of the cross-product, $\bold v\times\bold B$, *upward* in this case. 

Since $\bold F$ points upward too, $q$ must be positive.

The correct answer is:

$\boxed{1.0}\ \text C$

assessment_items.question_content:
A point charge, moving *south*, enters a region of uniform magnetic field with a speed of $3.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}$. It immediately experiences a $3.6\ \text{mN}$ force in the *upward* direction.

The magnetic field has a magnitude, $0.60\ \text{mT}$, toward *west*.

**What is the charge of the particle?**    
*Note: $\text{mN}=$ millinewtons, $\text{mT}=$ milliteslas.  [[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text C$
hints:
##Strategy

The force on the charge, $\bold F$, is related to the charge, $q$, its velocity, $\bold v$, and the magnetic field, $\bold B$, as,

$\begin{align}
\bold F&=q(\bold v\times\bold B)
\end{align}$

Let's note that:

- $\bold v$ is along south and $\bold B$ is along west, so $\bold v\perp\bold B$.

- $q$ is positive if $\bold F$ is in the same direction as the vector product, $\bold v\times\bold B$, and negative if $\bold F$ points opposite to $\bold v\times\bold B$.

##Solution

**Magnitude of $q$:**

$\begin{align}
|q|&=\dfrac{|\bold F|}{|\bold v\times\bold B|}\\\\
&=\dfrac{|\bold\redD F|}{|\bold\blue v||\bold\purpleD B|\sin90\degree}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redD{3.6\times10^{-3}\ \text N}}{\blue{3.0\ \dfrac{\text m}{\text s}}\cdot\purpleD{0.60\times10^{-3}\ \text T}}\\\\
&=2.0\ \text C
\end{align}$

We still need to check if $q$ is $+2.0\ \text C$ or $-2.0\ \text C$.

**Sign of $q$:**

*Right-hand rule:* With our right hand, let's point the -

- index finger along $\bold v$ (*south*),

- middle-finger along $\bold B$ (*west*).

The stretched thumb gives the direction of the cross-product, $\bold v\times\bold B$, *downward* in this case. 

Since $\bold F$ points upward, $q$ must be negative.

The correct answer is:

$\boxed{-2.0}\ \text C$
